title: - CONTEXTUAL FUSION FOR ADVERSARIAL ROBUST NESS [PDF]

paragraphs.text:
Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

## - CONTEXTUAL FUSION FOR ADVERSARIAL ROBUST NESS

## **Anonymous authors**

Paper under double-blind review

## ABSTRACT

Mammalian brains handle complex reasoning tasks in a gestalt manner by integrating information from regions of the brain that are specialised to individual sensory modalities. This allows for improved robustness and better generalisation ability. In contrast, deep neural networks are usually designed to process one particular information stream and susceptible to various types of adversarial perturbations. While many methods exist for detecting and defending against adversarial attacks, they do not generalise across a range of attacks and negatively affect performance on clean, unperturbed data. We developed a fusion model using a combination of background and foreground features extracted in parallel from Places-CNN and Imagenet-CNN. We tested the benefits of the fusion approach on preserving adversarial robustness for human perceivable (e.g., Gaussian blur) and network perceivable (e.g., gradient-based) attacks for CIFAR-10 and MS COCO data sets. For gradient based attacks, our results show that fusion allows for significant improvements in classification without decreasing performance on unperturbed data and without need to perform adversarial retraining. Our fused model revealed improvements for Gaussian blur type perturbations as well. The increase in performance from fusion approach depended on the variability of the image contexts; larger increases were seen for classes of images with larger differences in their contexts. We also demonstrate the effect of regularization to bias the classifier decision in the presence of a known adversary. We propose that this biologically inspired approach to integrate information across multiple modalities provides a new way to improve adversarial robustness that can be complementary to current state of the art approaches.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

## 1.1 BIOLOGICAL BACKGROUND

Current deep learning networks are designed to optimally solve specific learning tasks for a particular category of inputs (e.g., convolutional neural networks (CNNs) for visual pattern recognition), but are limited in their ability to solve tasks that require combining different feature categories (e.g., visual, semantic, auditory) into one coherent representation. Some of the challenges include finding the right alignment of unimodal representations, fusion strategy, and complexity measures for determining the efficacy of fused representationsBaltrusaitis et al. (2017). In comparison, biological systems are excellent in their ability to form unique and coherent object representations, which is usually done in the associative cortex, by linking together different object features available from different specialized cortical networks, e.g., primary visual or auditory cortices Gisiger et al. (2000); Pandya & Seltzer (1982); Mars et al. (2017); Rosen et al. (2017). This natural strategy has many advantages including better discrimination performance, stability against adversarial attacks and better scalability Gilad & Helmchen (2020). Indeed, if a classification decision is made based on a combination of features from different sensory categories, a noise or lack of information in one category can be compensated by another to make a correct decision. Furthermore, different types of sensory information can complement each other by being available at different times within a processing window. A good example may be human driving skill which relies on a combination of visual and auditory processing that helps to avoid mistakes and greatly enhances performance over only vision-based driving. Another example is insect navigation that depends both on visual and olfactory

information to minimize classification error and to identify objects more reliably across range of distancesStrube-Bloss & RÃ¶ssler (2018).

Although it seems obvious that humans and animals base their classification decisions on the complex mixture of features from different modalities using specialized classifiers in each of them, this ability is still lacking in current state of the art machine learning (ML) algorithms. Problems include difficulty of training because of the lack of data sets combining different types of information, and suboptimal performance of generic deep learning networks vs specialized ones. Indeed, e.g., high performance of the CNNs designed for visual processing depends on their architecture that makes explicit assumptions that inputs are images, and the same network performs poorly for other types of data. Thus, there is a need to develop approaches that would combine strength of specialized networks with ability to integrate information across multiple streams as human and animal brain can do efficiently.

## 1.2 MULTI-MODAL FUSION

Multimodal fusion has been previously explored for hard classification problems. Proposed methods in literature include learning joint representations from unimodal representations that are derived using VLADGong et al. (2014), Fisher Vector representationsDixit et al. (2015), and deep features locally extracted from CNN’s with various configurationsWu et al. (2015)Yoo et al. (2015)Shen et al. (2019). These approaches have been successfully applied for action, scene and event recognition, and object detection tasks.

In Zhou et al. (2014), the authors used features extracted from Alexnet pretrained on Places365 and Imagenet to show how internal representations of these networks perform for various scene and object centric datasets. Performance was not significantly superior to using a unimodal approach, however, some of the advantages were found to be related to reducing data bias. In Herranz et al. (2016) the authors demonstrated this by using combinations of object and scene features aggregated at different scales to build a more efficient joint representation that helped to mitigate dataset bias induced by scale. Our fusion approach aligns most closely with the methods proposed in these papers.

## 1.3 ADVERSARIAL ATTACKS

Image processing using deep convolutional neural nets (CNNs) has made historical leaps in the last decade Krizhevsky et al. (2012); He et al. (2016); Szegedy et al. (2015). However, the same convolutional networks are susceptible to small perturbations in data, even imperceptible to humans, that can result in misclassification. There have been two main approaches for investigating ANN robustness: adversarial machine learning and training data manipulation Ford et al. (2019). Although it has been proposed that adversarial and manipulation robustness can be increased through various mechanisms during the training phase, recent research has shown that these methods are mostly ineffective or their effectiveness is inconclusive Uesato et al. (2018) Geirhos et al. (2018); Athalye et al. (2018).

Fast Gradient Sign Method (FGSM)Goodfellow et al. (2014) is a popular one-step attack that is easier to defend compared to the iterative variants like Basic Iterative Method(BIM)Kurakin et al. (2016) or Projected Gradient Descent (PGD). Adversarial training and its variants are defense methods commonly employed for dealing with adversarial attacks. In Tramèr et al. (2017) the authors found that adversarial training is more robust with adversarial examples generated from white box attacks (attacks designed against the specifics of the underlying CNN architecture and weights) but it remains vulnerable to black box transferred examples (examples generated in architecture agnostic manner). To combat this, an ensemble model was proposed that combines adversarial examples created from different source models and substitute pretrained networks. In general, adversarial training on one type of attack does not generalise to other attacks and can compromise classification accuracy on clean, unperturbed data. For example, in Kurakin et al. (2017) the authors demonstrated that adversarial retraining on one step attacks do not protect against iterative attacks like PGD. Consequently, adversarial training with multi step attack is regarded as the state of the art method used for improving adversarial robustness for white box and black box attacks and was initially proposed in Madry et al. (2017). Recently, Wong et al. (2020) showed that single-step adversarial training with an attack similar to FGSM successfully yields models robust to white-box attacks, if the stepsizes of the attack’s random and gradient step are appropriately tuned. Several other methods are

## 1.4 SUMMARY OF OUR APPROACH

In this paper, we describe the fusion of two data streams, one focused on background (context) and another focused on the foreground (object) image information, and we use different types of adversarial perturbations to evaluate the efficacy of the fused representation.

We explore the following main concepts:

- Adversarial attacks can have divergent effects on context feature space and object feature space.
- Utilizing combination of multiple modalities for the information processing can be an efficient method for combating adversarial attacks.
- Context features provide additional information to object-oriented data, and can be used to improve classification, especially during adversarial attacks.

## 2 METHODOLOGY

## 2.1 CONTEXTUAL FUSION

We developed three different image classifiers designed to extract foreground features, background features, or a fused version of both. Below we refer to these as the foreground, background, and joint classifiers, respectively. The distinction between the various classifiers is based on the underlying training data. The foreground classifier was trained on the object-centric Imagenet database, whereas the background classifier was trained on the scene-centric Places365 database similar to Zhou et al. (2014). Each classifier was built on the Resnet18 architecture He et al. (2016) with a final fully connected layer specific to the dataset (Figure 1). Only the 512 dimensional fully connected layer was finetuned for MS COCO Lin et al. (2015) or CIFAR-10 Krizhevsky & Hinton (2009). For the joint classifier, we adopted a late fusion strategy and concatenated features obtained from the foreground and background pre-trained networks. This 1024 dimensional representation was finetuned for the specific dataset.

## 2.2 DATASETS

MS COCO dataset is a large dataset with 1.6 million images that have multiple objects and multiple instances with overlapping contexts. We pruned the MS COCO dataset for images with fewer than two instances (<=2) of a single object so as to minimise cases of co-occurring context. We used available bounding boxes to constrain the percentage of foreground to be less than or equal to fifty percent of the total image, where foreground was defined by the area within the bounding box. This significantly reduced the dataset size but helped create the conditions for learning a representation

We also used CIFAR-10, which is a standard object recognition dataset with 10 classes. For experiments with CIFAR-10, we used the entire dataset with 50000 images for training and tested with the remaining 10000 images. All images from have been resized and cropped to 224x224 and normalized with CIFAR-10 mean and standard deviation.

## 2.3 ADVERSARIAL ATTACKS

We tested the classifiers with two different types of adversarial attacks to evaluate the benefits of fusion: Gaussian blur and FGSM. Gaussian blur was applied to the test set by convolving a portion of the image within the object bounding box given in the dataset with Gaussian kernel. Differing degrees of blur were created by varying the standard deviation of the kernel. Adversarial example attacks were generated using FGSM similar to Szegedy et al. (2013); Goodfellow et al. (2014). Here, small amounts of noise were added to the test images based on the gradient of the loss function with respect to the input. Following Goodfellow et al. (2014) we used the equation
where _ϵ_ is a small real number, _J_ is the loss as a function of the parameters ( _θ_ ), the input ( _x_ ), and the label _y_ .

## 3 RESULTS

## 3.1 BLUR AFFECTS FOREGROUND AND BACKGROUND CHANNELS DIFFERENTLY

The impact of adversarial attacks can vary with the CNN architecture and the underlying training data Dodge & Karam (2016). Here, we used two distinct CNNs with the same Resnet18 architecture to serve as the background and foreground feature extractors. The background feature extractor was trained on the Places365 database Zhou et al. (2014), a scene-centric collection of images with 365 scenes as categories (e.g. abbey, bedroom, and library). The foreground feature extractor was trained on the object-centric Imagenet database with object categories (e.g. goldfish, English setter, toaster). The two different feature extractors represent the same images in different ways and have a different sensitivity to the same adversarial attack. We use pretrained models readily available in Pytorch.

Blur is a natural artifact common to many real-world acquired images. Therefore, it is critical for an image classifier to maintain robustness to blur. We created blurred images by convolution of the foreground of each image with a Gaussian kernel. This type of Gaussian blur is perceivable by humans, and for small standard deviations (e.g. _σ_ = 0 _._ 001), the human visual system is able to perform a classification task with minimal mistakes. As we increase the amount of blur (e.g. _σ_ = 45), both humans and neural networks tend towards chance level performance. In our approach, only the foreground pixels are modified leaving the context of the images intact.

Figure 3: Effect of Gaussian Blur on classification performance for MS COCO data. Panels a,c,d show classification performance for different levels of blur ( _σ_ ). ‘All’ refers to the 24 classes from different supercategories that remain after downsizing the dataset. ‘Dissimilar’ and ‘Similar’ are eight classes randomly selected from the 24 classes such that the supercategories are either distinct or the same (see Methodology). b) Example of high Gaussian blur ( _σ_ = 45) on foreground pixels within bounding box.

We first tested effect of application of the Gaussian blur on images from the MS COCO dataset. The blur was applied to the bounding box area of the image, where main object was located. Images were processed by each of the two CNNs independently and features were extracted from the batch normalization layers. To illustrate effect of blur on the high-level foreground and background features, we visualized the feature space using PCA to reduce dimensionality for few representative image classes (see Figure 2). We found that blur had differing effects between the background (scenecentric) and foreground (object-centric) features. For the foreground features, blur caused a shift of the entire statistical representation subspace, i.e., all blurred images moved conjointly away from the non-blurred images (compare small vs large dots in Figure 2). However, for background features, blurred images were represented in the same statistical subspace as clean images. This shows, as one can expect, that blurring foreground (object) alone will have larger impact on the features extracted by the network focused on the foreground than on the features coming from the network that was train to recognize a background. This finding supports an idea that combined use of the foreground and background classifiers to process images in a multi-modal fashion may help to defend against adversarial attacks designed against specific image components. Specifically, if adversarial attacks affected only one channel of information (i.e. the foreground channel), then multi-modal integration could overcome these attacks.

## 3.2 GAUSSIAN BLUR AND THE EFFECT OF CONTEXTUAL FUSION

To directly test the hypothesis about increased robustness of the fusion based classifier against blur, we compared classification performance of the foreground, background, and fused classifiers on the images where foreground object was blurred. We found an increase in classification for all levels of _σ_ with the largest increase over foreground classifier for _σ >_ 5 (Figure 3a).

Because, our joint fusion method depends on associating context with object, we also tested separately images with Similar and Dissimilar background context. To do this, we used the "supercategories" given in the MS COCO dataset to refine test sets into a "Similar test set" where all images came from different categories but the same supercategory (e.g. "dog", "horse", and "sheep" - all animals) and the "Dissimilar test set" where images come from distinct supercategories (e.g. "dog"-animal, "airplane"vehicles, and "toilet"-indoor). This changes our problem to either a coarse grained classification e.g. classes from different supercategories like “Animals”, “Vehicles”, “Indoor”, “Outdoor”, or a much harder fine grained classification, e.g. classes from the same supercategory of “Animals”.

An increase in classification was evident for each subset of the MS COCO dataset (Similar or Dissimilar) and almost all values of _σ_ (Figure 3c and Figure 3d). The largest improvements for the joint classifier were seen for moderate values of _σ_ and while using the Dissimilar test set (at _σ_ = 10 the joint classifier outperforms the foreground classifier by 10%, Figure 3d). Thus, if all images in the test set contained different contexts (e.g. some were indoor images and others were outdoor images), then the joint classier performed much better than the individual ones. This finding was expected because contexts in the dissimilar test set contain more information specific to the underlying class. However, even when using the Similar test set, we found significant increases in classification over foreground alone. For example, classification with the joint classifier was 5% higher at _σ_ = 10.

## 3.3 GRADIENT-BASED ATTACKS

Adversarial attacks were constructed based on adding the right type of noise that maximizes the increase in the loss function, gradient-based attacks Szegedy et al. (2013). The magnitude of the added noise was quantified by _ϵ_ (see Methodology and Goodfellow et al. (2014)). Here, we explored how well a fusion strategy can defend against them. Adversarial images were created from the full original images using FGSM method Goodfellow et al. (2014) applied to the foreground network, and performance was tested with the foreground, background and joint networks.

For MS COCO dataset with all 24 classes (the ’All’ test set in Figure 3), we found that classification accuracy for the foreground classifier quickly dropped to chance with increasing _ϵ_ - strength of the attack (Figure 4a). The background classifier degraded at a much slower rate compared to the foreground classifier. A joint classifier revealed somewhat intermediate level of performance across a range of the attack strengths. Importantly, joint classifier revealed the same level of performance as the foreground one for intact images, suggesting that our approach can overcome a common problem of the adversarial defense - degraded performance for intact images (Figure 4a). To reveal relative contribution of two networks (object-centric and scene-centric) to the joint classifier, we examined the weights of the trained joint classifier (Figure 4b) and found that the foreground and background networks influenced the joint decision almost equally. Qualitatively, this suggests that since the MS COCO dataset has object information embedded with semantically meaningful context, the background classifier was able to weigh in on the joint decision, thereby retaining performance above foreground network when it was affected by adversarial attack.

We next tested our fusion strategy on CIFAR-10 (Figure 4c). This dataset is much larger than the subset of MS COCO dataset we used, and it has established performance baselines in literature for different types of adversarial defensesWong et al. (2020), Yan et al. (2018). For the foreground classifier, we trained all layers of a Resnet18 on CIFAR-10, with weights initialized from a Resnet 18 pretrained on Imagenet. For the background classifier, we train only the last fully connected layer of Resnet18 with weights initialized from a Resnet18 pretrained on the Places365 dataset. This was done to ensure the background feature extractor represents scene-centric information, without overwriting to the object-based features in CIFAR-10. The joint classifier was a concatenated model of the foreground and background classifiers as described above such that only the last fully connected layer was trained and the model parameters of the foreground and background classifiers were retained. We found that the adversarial examples crafted using the foreground classifier also affected the joint classifier with its performance being only marginally above the performance of the foreground

classifier. We further examined the weights of the joint classifier after training (Figure 4d) which revealed that the foreground features dominated the joint decision. This was most likely due to the fact that the background classifier significantly under-performed the foreground classifier at _ϵ_ = 0 ( 60% vs 85%). The last finding was not surprising because of the object-centric nature of the CIFAR-10 dataset, and the lack of meaningful background information. Thus, without significant input from background features, multimodal fusion failed to increase performance in FGSM attacks.

These results suggest that although adversarial examples do transfer across classifiers, a principled way to combine contextual information can be useful for adversarial robustness without the added cost of adversarial retraining and compromising accuracy on unperturbed data. This usefulness depended on two factors: a) the modalities needed to be sufficiently distinct; and b) the fusion mechanism needed to balance contribution of the information streams from different modalities. Below, we explored how this balance can be struck in an optimal way.

## 3.4 REGULARIZATION ON KNOWN ADVERSARY

When it is known that the foreground is targeted by the attack, considerable gains can be made using the multi-modal method, even when compared to conventional methods like adversarial retraining. Inspired by the finding that improvement in the joint network is related to the relative weighting of background information, we employed regularization of the targeted foreground network to enhance the performance of the joint network. The equation below regularized the foreground weights of the joint network with L2 penalty determined by a tuneable hyperparameter _α_ .

The first term was the standard cross entropy loss between the activation vector _ai_ and the target vector _ti_ for all N samples from C classes. The second term was the L2 penalty on the foreground weights, _θfg_ . This allowed the network to bias the classifier decision towards the background or the foreground based on the value of _α_ .

We also compared the performance of the regularized joint network with a standard method to combat FGSM attacks, adversarial retraining Madry et al. (2017). We found that tuning _α_ had a strong effect on the performance of the joint network (Figure 5). The highest levels of regularization ( _α_ = 10) enabled the joint classifier to perform close to the levels of the background classifier while retaining high performance on the clean test set. Since there was a known adversary on the foreground, the biasing mechanism described in the loss function equation above, can be used to weight the contextual decision of the background classifier more than the foreground. Surprisingly, the joint network with higher regulation outperformed the adversarial retraining strategy at all levels of _ϵ_ . It suggests that foreground regularization allows the user to select the degree of a more informative object-centric channel as opposed to a scene-centric channel that is less sensitive to the attack.

## 4 CONCLUSION

We present a novel method using semantic data fusion to increase robustness to adversarial attacks. By using features from distinct information streams, object-centered foreground and scene-centered background, we were able to maintain higher classification performance in the face of targeted adversarial attacks. We found that the degree of success for this method partially depends on the amount of variability in the background data. Thus, our method was more successful at maintaining robustness when objects came from distinct super categories of data with different and distinct backgrounds. Regularization of the foreground network enhanced this performance far above standard adversarial training strategies. In this work, we used a simple method of fusion - a single fully connected layer integrating the outputs of the object-centric and scene-centric networks. Our approach can be easily scaled by (a) adding other richer contexts and modalities (e.g., auditory, text) and (b) by implementing more sophisticated fusion layers inspired by neuro-scientific computational principles (e.g. recurrent networks, etc).
Our work may lead to better understanding of how knowledge is extracted from experience in biological networks and how brain dynamics are shaped by the development of a rich internal model

of the world, including the ability to predict the outcomes of current situations and one’s own actions in that context. The sophistication and complexity of the brain processing to combine different types of information streams to create internal model of the world is arguably the basis for "cognitive reserve", which is a significant factor in protection from age and disease related dementia, and so a deep understanding of how it is created and expressed is of high societal impact. Equally important, our study may provide insights into the algorithms that evolution has devised to make predictions about optimal behaviour based on the multimodal rich input from the world. This is a main issue in machine learning and using insight from biology will undoubtedly lead to advances in machine learning algorithms.

progressive__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-previ.label: Progressive (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
progressive__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-previ.overall_feedback: This paper presents an intriguing, biologically inspired premise—leveraging multimodal fusion of "background" (Places-CNN) and "foreground" (Imagenet-CNN) features to bolster adversarial robustness without the performance degradation typically associated with traditional adversarial training. While the motivation is conceptually sound and touches upon a significant gap in current deep learning literature—specifically the lack of generalizable defense mechanisms—the current draft suffers from a lack of technical precision and rigorous grounding. The primary issue is a "methodological blur": the introduction over-relies on high-level biological analogies (e.g., insect navigation and mammalian cortical processing) without sufficiently bridging these metaphors to the specific architecture or fusion mechanism employed. Furthermore, the discussion of "modalities" is conceptually imprecise here; the paper treats background and foreground features as distinct "modalities," yet these are ostensibly derived from the same source image, potentially confusing feature-level aggregation with true multi-modal fusion. To improve the paper, the authors must shift focus from broad biological narrative to a more rigorous, formal definition of how this fusion architecture navigates the trade-off between clean-data accuracy and adversarial robustness, particularly defining why two CNN streams from related image domains provide a superior defense compared to standard ensemble or multi-stage feature extraction techniques.

comments.title: Missing Mathematical Formula for FGSM
comments.quote: Following Goodfellow et al. (2014) we used the equation...
comments.explanation: The methodology section references an equation to define the FGSM attack but fails to include the actual mathematical expression. This is a critical omission for reproducibility, as the paper should be self-contained regarding the specific implementation used.

comments.title: Unsubstantiated Claim Regarding Regularization
comments.quote: We also demonstrate the effect of regularization to bias the classifier decision in the presence of a known adversary.
comments.explanation: The authors claim to demonstrate the effect of regularization to bias classifier decisions; however, the provided methodology and results lack a description of this mechanism or data supporting its efficacy. The experimental section does not clarify how varying the regularization hyperparameter $\alpha$ facilitates dynamic bias against specific adversaries.

comments.title: Ambiguous Definition of Sigma in Blur Experiment
comments.quote: For example, classification with the joint classifier was 5% higher at $\sigma = 10$.
comments.explanation: The values provided for $\sigma$ (ranging from $0.001$ to $45$) are inconsistent relative to standard image processing parameters for $224 \times 224$ images. It is unclear if these values represent pixel-wise standard deviations of the Gaussian kernel or a normalized scaling factor. Without a clear definition, the blur intensity is not reproducible.

comments.title: Vague Description of Input Normalization and Preprocessing
comments.quote: All images from have been resized and cropped to 224x224 and normalized with CIFAR-10 mean and standard deviation.
comments.explanation: The normalization procedure is problematic because the model uses a ResNet18 backbone pre-trained on ImageNet, which typically requires ImageNet-specific constants, not those of CIFAR-10. Additionally, resizing 32x32 CIFAR-10 images to 224x224 introduces significant interpolation artifacts, which may confound the researchers' findings regarding the dataset's 'lack of meaningful background information'.

comments.title: Missing Formal Loss Function Definition
comments.quote: The equation below regularized the foreground weights of the joint network with L2 penalty determined by a tuneable hyperparameter $\alpha$.
comments.explanation: The regularization loss function and its mathematical relationship with the cross-entropy term are omitted. Though the text describes components (L2 penalty on foreground weights), it fails to provide the formal expression, rendering the core contribution of the 'known adversary' section underspecified.

comments.title: Inconsistent Claim regarding Robustness vs Adversarial Training
comments.quote: Surprisingly, the joint network with higher regulation outperformed the adversarial retraining strategy at all levels of $\epsilon$.
comments.explanation: The claim of broad superiority over adversarial training is not well-supported, as results are only presented for specific hyperparameters and a single attack type. The paper fails to address how this simple regularization compares against stronger, iterative attacks like PGD, nor does it acknowledge the potential shift in robustness if the 'background' classifier is not itself robust to the adversarial noise.

comments.title: Inconsistent Description of Dataset Partitioning and Coverage
comments.quote: Our final dataset comprised of 24 classes with 7500 images.
comments.explanation: The paper uses inconsistent definitions of the MS COCO subset. It is unclear if later mentions of 'All' classes refer to the 24-class pruned set or the full original dataset. This allows for potential misinterpretation of the results' generalizability.

comments.title: Potential Contradiction in Dataset Pruning Logic
comments.quote: We pruned the MS COCO dataset for images with fewer than two instances (<=2) of a single object
comments.explanation: The intersection of the text 'fewer than two' and the parenthetical '(<=2)' is contradictory. It is mathematically unclear whether the authors excluded images with 1 object or 2 objects.

progressive_original__gemini-3.1-flash-l.label: Progressive Original (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
progressive_original__gemini-3.1-flash-l.overall_feedback: This paper presents an intriguing, biologically inspired premise—leveraging multimodal fusion of "background" (Places-CNN) and "foreground" (Imagenet-CNN) features to bolster adversarial robustness without the performance degradation typically associated with traditional adversarial training. While the motivation is conceptually sound and touches upon a significant gap in current deep learning literature—specifically the lack of generalizable defense mechanisms—the current draft suffers from a lack of technical precision and rigorous grounding. The primary issue is a "methodological blur": the introduction over-relies on high-level biological analogies (e.g., insect navigation and mammalian cortical processing) without sufficiently bridging these metaphors to the specific architecture or fusion mechanism employed. Furthermore, the discussion of "modalities" is conceptually imprecise here; the paper treats background and foreground features as distinct "modalities," yet these are ostensibly derived from the same source image, potentially confusing feature-level aggregation with true multi-modal fusion. To improve the paper, the authors must shift focus from broad biological narrative to a more rigorous, formal definition of how this fusion architecture navigates the trade-off between clean-data accuracy and adversarial robustness, particularly defining why two CNN streams from related image domains provide a superior defense compared to standard ensemble or multi-stage feature extraction techniques.

comments.title: Missing FGSM Equation Definition
comments.quote:
Following Goodfellow et al. (2014) we used the equation

where $\epsilon$ is a small real number, $J$ is the loss as a function of the parameters ( $\theta$ ), the input ( $x$ ), and the label $y$ .
comments.explanation: The text introduces a section dedicated to the FGSM (Fast Gradient Sign Method) equation, but the actual mathematical expression is entirely missing from the document, appearing only as a blank space between the introductory sentence and the description of the variables. This is a critical technical omission, as the methodology section must be reproducible. While the reference to Goodfellow et al. (2014) is provided, the paper should be self-contained regarding the specific implementation used.

comments.title: Unsubstantiated Claim Regarding Regularization
comments.quote: We also demonstrate the effect of regularization to bias the classifier decision in the presence of a known adversary.
comments.explanation: In the Abstract, the authors claim to demonstrate the effect of regularization to bias the classifier decision. However, in the provided methodology and results sections, there is no mention of a regularization technique or a specific experiment involving bias toward known adversaries. This appears to be a dangling claim that is not supported by the experimental data presented in the paper.

comments.title: Inconsistent Description of Dataset Partitioning
comments.quote: Our final dataset comprised of 24 classes with 7500 images. We used 75% of the dataset for training and the rest for testing.
comments.explanation: Calculating the split: 75% of 7500 is 5625 for training and 1875 for testing. This is mathematically consistent. However, the subsequent sentence says: 'For experiments with CIFAR-10, we used the entire dataset with 50000 images for training and tested with the remaining 10000 images.' This sum (60,000) corresponds to the standard CIFAR-10 training/test split, but the phrasing is slightly ambiguous as to whether they are using the training set for validation or if they simply mean they used the standard partitions. While likely clear to a domain expert, the juxtaposition between the custom MS COCO split and the standard CIFAR split could be clearer.

comments.title: Ambiguous Definition of Sigma in Blur Experiment
comments.quote: For example, classification with the joint classifier was 5% higher at $\sigma = 10$.
comments.explanation: Earlier in section 3.1, the authors state: 'For small standard deviations (e.g. $\sigma = 0.001$)... (e.g. $\sigma = 45$)'. It is unclear if $\sigma$ refers to the standard deviation of the Gaussian kernel in pixels or a normalized value. Given that the image is 224x224, a $\sigma$ of 45 is massive. Without specifying whether this is pixels or a fraction of the image dimension, the results are difficult to interpret or replicate.

comments.title: Missing Mathematical Formula for FGSM
comments.quote:
Following Goodfellow et al. (2014) we used the equation

where $\epsilon$ is a small real number, $J$ is the loss as a function of the parameters ( $\theta$ ), the input ( $x$ ), and the label $y$.
comments.explanation: The text references that an equation will be provided to define the FGSM attack, but the actual LaTeX formula is completely missing from the provided passage. While an expert reader might infer the formula (x' = x + \epsilon * sign(\nabla_x J(\theta, x, y))), the paper as written is technically incomplete at a crucial methodology juncture, making it impossible to verify the specific implementation of the attack used in the experiments.

comments.title: Vague Description of Input Normalization in CIFAR-10
comments.quote: All images from have been resized and cropped to 224x224 and normalized with CIFAR-10 mean and standard deviation.
comments.explanation: The passage states that images were resized and cropped to 224x224 for 'All images', but then mentions normalized with 'CIFAR-10 mean and standard deviation'. Since the foreground model is a ResNet18 initialized from ImageNet, standard protocol usually requires ImageNet-specific normalization constants rather than CIFAR-10 constants. Furthermore, the phrasing 'All images from have been' contains an obvious grammatical gap, which, combined with the ambiguous normalization procedure, leaves the preprocessing pipeline for the foreground/background streams underspecified.

comments.title: Potential Contradiction in Dataset Pruning Logic
comments.quote: We pruned the MS COCO dataset for images with fewer than two instances (<=2) of a single object
comments.explanation: The phrasing 'fewer than two instances (<=2)' is contradictory. 'Fewer than two' means <2 (i.e., 1 or 0), while '(<=2)' refers to 2 or fewer. If the authors kept images with 2, 3, etc., objects, they should have said 'fewer than two' or '<2'. If they filtered out images with >2 objects, the logic described in the paper is ambiguous. While context implies they want multiple objects to provide context, the specific inclusion criteria are mathematically unclear.

comments.title: Inconsistent claim regarding CIFAR-10 background information
comments.quote: This was not surprising because of the object-centric nature of the CIFAR-10 dataset, and the lack of meaningful background information. Thus, without significant input from background features, multimodal fusion failed to increase performance in FGSM attacks.
comments.explanation: The authors claim that CIFAR-10 contains a 'lack of meaningful background information,' which explains the failure of the fusion strategy. However, the methodology (Section 2.2) states that all images were resized/cropped to 224x224 and normalized. Given that CIFAR-10 images are natively 32x32, resizing them to 224x224 (the input size for ResNet18 pre-trained on ImageNet/Places365) introduces significant interpolation artifacts and effectively alters what the network perceives as 'background' vs 'foreground'. The failure of the fusion model may be due to the architectural mismatch/upstream processing rather than an inherent property of the CIFAR-10 dataset itself. This framing is technically misleading as it shifts the blame to the dataset's composition rather than the experimental setup.

comments.title: Ambiguity in 'All' dataset size vs. reported MS COCO subset
comments.quote: Our final dataset comprised of 24 classes with 7500 images.
comments.explanation: In Section 2.2, the authors define the MS COCO subset as 24 classes with 7500 images. Later, in Section 3.3 and Figure 4, they refer to 'All' classes in the context of MS COCO. It is unclear if 'All' refers to the aforementioned 24-class pruned set or the full original MS COCO set. If it refers to the latter, the comparison in Figure 4a would be inconsistent with the methodology described in 2.2. Context suggests 'All' refers to the 24 classes, but the phrasing is ambiguous and could lead a reader to incorrectly assume the results generalize to the entire diverse MS COCO distribution.

comments.title: Insufficient justification for Joint Classifier weight dominance
comments.quote: The last finding was not surprising because of the object-centric nature of the CIFAR-10 dataset, and the lack of meaningful background information.
comments.explanation: The authors attribute the dominance of foreground weights in the CIFAR-10 joint classifier (Figure 4d) to the dataset's nature. However, they also note that the background classifier performed significantly worse on clean data (60% vs 85%). In a standard linear fusion layer (late fusion), the model will naturally learn to assign higher weights to the more accurate modality. The authors do not sufficiently distinguish whether the foreground dominance is due to the 'nature of the dataset' (semantic content) or simply due to the performance gap on the clean task, which is a known artifact of training joint classifiers with heterogeneous unimodal base-accuracy.

comments.title: Missing Loss Function Definition
comments.quote: The equation below regularized the foreground weights of the joint network with L2 penalty determined by a tuneable hyperparameter $\alpha$ .
comments.explanation: The text references 'The equation below' to define the regularization loss function, yet the equation itself is missing from the provided text block. While the paper describes the components of the loss (cross-entropy and L2 penalty on foreground weights), it does not explicitly state the mathematical relationship between them. Since this regularization is a core contribution of the 'known adversary' section, the omission of the formal expression makes the methodology underspecified.

comments.title: Unspecified Regularization Term definition
comments.quote: The first term was the standard cross entropy loss between the activation vector $a_i$ and the target vector $t_i$ for all $N$ samples from $C$ classes. The second term was the L2 penalty on the foreground weights, $\theta_{fg}$ .
comments.explanation: The text refers to the 'second term' as an L2 penalty on $\theta_{fg}$. In standard machine learning literature, an L2 penalty is typically denoted as $\lambda||w||^2$. The text describes the penalty but does not define the mathematical form (e.g., whether it is a squared sum of weights multiplied by the hyperparameter $\alpha$). Without the specific formula, the interaction between $\alpha$ and the foreground weights is ambiguous.

comments.title: Inconsistent Claim regarding Robustness vs Adversarial Training
comments.quote: Surprisingly, the joint network with higher regulation outperformed the adversarial retraining strategy at all levels of $\epsilon$ .
comments.explanation: The authors claim that their regularization method outperforms adversarial retraining 'at all levels of $\epsilon$' (e.g., 0 to 0.3 in Figure 5). However, Figure 5 only presents results for specific $\alpha$ values and one attack type. Comparing a novel regularization technique—which essentially biases the model toward a pre-trained background classifier—to a full adversarial training approach (which involves retraining the internal weights) is a strong claim. The paper does not provide enough evidence (e.g., against iterative attacks like PGD) to justify this broad superiority, especially since the 'background' classifier might not be robust to the same adversarial noise as an adversarially trained model.

comments.title: Undefined Symbol in Methodology
comments.quote: Following Goodfellow et al. (2014) we used the equation where $\epsilon$ is a small real number, $J$ is the loss as a function of the parameters ( $\theta$ ), the input ( $x$ ), and the label $y$.
comments.explanation: The text introduces the FGSM update rule description but omits the actual equation ($x_{adv} = x + \epsilon \cdot \text{sign}(\nabla_x J(\theta, x, y))$). While it references the seminal paper, for a methodology section, the complete formula should be provided. In the provided text, it simply says 'we used the equation' without providing the display equation itself.

comments.title: Use of undefined variable in FGSM equation
comments.quote:
Following Goodfellow et al. (2014) we used the equation

where $\epsilon$ is a small real number, $J$ is the loss as a function of the parameters ( $\theta$ ), the input ( $x$ ), and the label $y$ .
comments.explanation: The text references an equation but fails to include it, resulting in a gap where the mathematical definition should be. While the variable $\epsilon$ is defined and the components of the loss function $J$ are described, the actual expression (e.g., $x' = x + \epsilon \cdot \text{sign}(\nabla_x J(\theta, x, y))$) is missing. Given that FGSM is a standard technique, an expert reader can infer the intent; however, the omission makes the methodology section technically incomplete as it does not specify the exact adversarial update used.

comments.title: Potentially misleading claim regarding regularization
comments.quote: We also demonstrate the effect of regularization to bias the classifier decision in the presence of a known adversary.
comments.explanation: The authors claim to demonstrate the effect of regularization to bias decisions; however, the provided summary and methodology sections introduce $\alpha$ as a hyperparameter for foreground weighting, but do not actually describe the mechanism or provide results on how varying $\alpha$ allows for dynamic bias against specific adversaries. The paper discusses using background features for robustness, but the specific experimental demonstration of 'regularization to bias the classifier' as a defense mechanism appears underspecified in the provided text.

comments.title: Inconsistent notation usage for Gaussian blur intensity
comments.quote: For example, classification with the joint classifier was 5% higher at $\sigma = 10$.
comments.explanation: Earlier in the text, the authors define Gaussian blur intensity using $\sigma$ (e.g., $\sigma = 0.001$, $\sigma = 45$). However, they also mention 'A filter with Gauss kernel and $\sigma = 5$ was used to blur images' in Figure 2's caption. The transition between using $\sigma$ as a standard deviation scalar in an equation vs. suggesting it as a discrete kernel parameter is slightly ambiguous. While context makes it clear they are referring to blur magnitude, the lack of a standardized scaling definition makes reproducing the exact 'moderate' or 'high' blur settings difficult.

progressive__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Progressive (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
progressive__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback: This paper proposes a biologically inspired framework for adversarial robustness by fusing foreground (ImageNet-CNN) and background (Places-CNN) feature streams, claiming zero-shot robustness gains against gradient-based and blur perturbations without adversarial retraining. While the motivation to exploit contextual information is well-articulated and the prose is generally clear, the work suffers from a fundamental conceptual mismatch in its core framing: the authors repeatedly invoke “multi-modal fusion” and analogize to sensory integration in mammalian brains, yet they actually combine two visual feature representations rather than distinct sensory modalities, which weakens both the biological analogy and the technical precision. Furthermore, categorizing Gaussian blur as an adversarial attack is misleading, as it functions more as a domain-shift or robustness benchmark than a targeted perturbation, and the strong claims regarding cross-dataset robustness improvements require rigorous baselines against contemporary, non-retraining defense methods to establish genuine novelty. Ultimately, while the structural hypothesis is intriguing, the paper would benefit from tighter terminology, a clearer alignment between the stated biological inspiration and the implemented visual-context fusion, and a more precise positioning within recent adversarial robustness literature to meet ICLR’s standards.

comments.title: Missing FGSM Equation in Methodology
comments.quote: Following Goodfellow et al. (2014) we used the equation where _ϵ_ is a small real number, _J_ is the loss as a function of the parameters ( _θ_ ), the input ( _x_ ), and the label _y_ .
comments.explanation: The authors explicitly state they used an equation for the Fast Gradient Sign Method (FGSM) but the formula itself is entirely omitted. I initially suspected a copy-paste error from the PDF extraction. After checking the surrounding text, I confirmed that while the variables (ϵ, J, θ, x, y) are defined, the core update rule is missing. Standard FGSM updates the input as $x_{adv} = x + \epsilon \cdot \text{sign}(\nabla_x J(\theta, x, y))$. Without the explicit equation, readers cannot verify the exact implementation details or reproduce the attack setup. This omission undermines methodological transparency and reproducibility.

comments.title: Contradictory Dataset Pruning Criterion
comments.quote: We pruned the MS COCO dataset for images with fewer than two instances (<=2) of a single object so as to minimise cases of co-occurring context.
comments.explanation: The phrase 'fewer than two instances' mathematically means strictly less than 2 (i.e., exactly 1 instance). However, the parenthetical clarification '(<=2)' indicates two or fewer instances (≤2). These two conditions contradict each other. I checked the methodology to see if the authors intended to completely remove multi-object images, but the ambiguous phrasing leaves the exact filtering threshold unclear. Since the dataset size and context variability directly support the paper's core hypothesis, this inconsistency limits reproducibility and creates doubt about the exact experimental setup.

comments.title: Broken Notation for Sigma Threshold
comments.quote: We found an increase in classification for all levels of _σ_ with the largest increase over foreground classifier for _σ >_ 5 (Figure 3a).
comments.explanation: The symbol '_σ >_ 5' appears to be a LaTeX spacing or OCR artifact. It clearly intends to convey the mathematical inequality $\sigma > 5$. While the intended meaning is unambiguous from context and Figure 3, the broken notation could momentarily confuse a careful reader parsing the conditional relationship. A simple correction to standard mathematical formatting would eliminate this minor distraction.

comments.title: Contradictory dataset filtering condition
comments.quote: We pruned the MS COCO dataset for images with fewer than two instances (<=2) of a single object so as to minimise cases of co-occurring context.
comments.explanation: I initially flagged this because the English phrasing and the mathematical notation directly contradict each other. The authors clearly intend to isolate single-object images to cleanly study how background context influences classification. However, 'fewer than two' strictly means `< 2` (i.e., exactly 1 instance), while the parenthetical `(<=2)` explicitly means `≤ 2` (one or two instances). I checked the methodology and the stated goal ('minimise cases of co-occurring context'), which strongly supports filtering for exactly one object, making the `<=2` notation likely a typo. Acknowledging that dataset curation is labor-intensive and the overall experimental design is sound, the unresolved contradiction could mislead readers regarding the exact dataset composition and the rigor of contextual isolation.

comments.title: Missing adversarial perturbation equation
comments.quote:
Following Goodfellow et al. (2014) we used the equation 
 
 where _ϵ_ is a small real number, _J_ is the loss as a function of the parameters ( _θ_ ), the input ( _x_ ), and the label _y_ .
comments.explanation: The manuscript explicitly references an equation for generating FGSM attacks, but the formula itself is completely absent. I initially suspected a standard PDF-to-text OCR drop, but the subsequent variable definitions confirm that the equation is structurally necessary for reproducibility. I acknowledge the authors correctly identify the standard references (Szegedy, Goodfellow) and describe the inputs to the loss function accurately. However, without the explicit update rule (typically $x_{adv} = x + \epsilon \cdot \text{sign}(\nabla_x J(\theta, x, y))$), a reader cannot verify critical implementation details such as gradient computation scope, sign handling, or pixel saturation/clipping. Even assuming the most plausible intended formula, its omission leaves the attack generation procedure underspecified.

comments.title: Implausible Gaussian blur standard deviation value
comments.quote: This type of Gaussian blur is perceivable by humans, and for small standard deviations (e.g. _σ_ = 0 _._ 001), the human visual system is able to perform a classification task with minimal mistakes.
comments.explanation: The authors cite $\sigma = 0.001$ as an example of a small blur where human classification performance remains near perfect. I initially wondered if this was intentional, but in standard image processing, a Gaussian kernel with $\sigma = 0.001$ is effectively an identity transform on a discrete pixel grid; it introduces negligible smoothing and is imperceptible. I checked Figure 2 and the surrounding text, which use $\sigma=5$ and $\sigma=45$, confirming that the intended experimental range is much larger. I acknowledge the core point about minimal blur not disrupting classification is valid, and the OCR artifact `0 _._ 001` suggests a formatting error. However, citing $0.001$ as a perceptible blur threshold is technically inaccurate and could mislead readers about the perturbation scale and the baseline for human/computer vision robustness.

comments.title: Incomplete sentence and ambiguous normalization scope
comments.quote: All images from have been resized and cropped to 224x224 and normalized with CIFAR-10 mean and standard deviation.
comments.explanation: The sentence is grammatically incomplete ('All images from [missing noun]'). More importantly, I noticed the authors state that normalization uses CIFAR-10 statistics. I checked the surrounding context, which immediately follows a description of the CIFAR-10 train/test split, and concluded the authors likely intended to say 'All images from [the CIFAR-10 experiments] have been...'. I acknowledge that standardizing inputs is routine, but applying CIFAR-10's color mean/std to Places365 and MS COCO is non-standard and typically requires explicit justification due to different color distributions. The missing noun creates ambiguity about whether this preprocessing was dataset-specific or universal, which matters for reproducibility and understanding potential feature extraction biases.

comments.title: Contradiction in foreground classifier training protocol for CIFAR-10
comments.quote: Only the 512 dimensional fully connected layer was finetuned for MS COCO Lin et al. (2015) or CIFAR-10 Krizhevsky & Hinton (2009). [Sec 2.1] ... For the foreground classifier, we trained all layers of a Resnet18 on CIFAR-10, with weights initialized from a Resnet 18 pretrained on Imagenet. [Sec 3.3]
comments.explanation: The methodology explicitly states that only the 512D fully connected layer is finetuned for both datasets. However, in Section 3.3, the authors describe training *all layers* of the foreground ResNet18 for the CIFAR-10 experiment. This is a direct methodological contradiction. I initially wondered if 'finetuned' in Sec 2.1 implied full training, but the explicit contrast in Sec 3.3 confirms a protocol change. The authors likely modified the training strategy for CIFAR-10 to match established baselines, but failed to reconcile this with the general methodology description. This gap complicates reproducibility and leaves the exact experimental setup ambiguous.

comments.title: Ambiguous specification of feature extraction layers
comments.quote: Images were processed by each of the two CNNs independently and features were extracted from the batch normalization layers.
comments.explanation: The passage states that features are extracted from 'the batch normalization layers.' ResNet18 contains numerous BN layers distributed across its residual blocks. Given that the paper consistently refers to a 512-dimensional representation (consistent with the penultimate layer output before the final FC layer), it is unclear which specific BN layer or feature map tensor is being referenced. While BN is technically applied to these features, naming 'the BN layers' as the extraction source is imprecise and underspecifies which exact activation map is concatenated during late fusion. A reader cannot confidently reconstruct the fusion tensor without guessing the architectural location.

comments.title: Inverted reasoning regarding background classifier transfer learning
comments.quote: This was done to ensure the background feature extractor represents scene-centric information, without overwriting to the object-based features in CIFAR-10.
comments.explanation: The authors justify freezing the backbone of the Places365-initialized background network to avoid 'overwriting object-based features in CIFAR-10.' This phrasing is logically inverted. Places365 is pre-trained on scene data, not object data. Training the final FC layer on CIFAR-10 (an object classification task) will inherently force the classifier head to learn object-level decision boundaries for the target classes. The plausible author intent is that freezing the backbone prevents overwriting the *pre-trained scene-centric features*. As written, the justification contradicts standard transfer learning principles and obscures the actual rationale for the freezing strategy, which is critical for understanding how the background channel retains contextual information under attack.

comments.title: Mathematical/Logical contradiction in dataset pruning criteria
comments.quote: We pruned the MS COCO dataset for images with fewer than two instances (<=2) of a single object so as to minimise cases of co-occurring context.
comments.explanation: The phrase 'fewer than two instances' mathematically denotes <2 (i.e., exactly 1 instance), while the parenthetical '(<=2)' explicitly includes 2 instances. This is a direct logical contradiction. Furthermore, the stated goal is to 'minimise cases of co-occurring context,' which strongly implies retaining only images with a single primary object. Allowing 2 instances would directly work against this objective. I checked standard MS COCO filtering practices, which typically isolate single-object images to ensure clean context-label alignment. The typo in the notation would confuse readers attempting to reproduce the dataset split.

comments.title: Ambiguous phrasing regarding feature preservation on CIFAR-10
comments.quote: This was done to ensure the background feature extractor represents scene-centric information, without overwriting to the object-based features in CIFAR-10.
comments.explanation: The sentence initially reads as logically incoherent because CIFAR-10 is a dataset, not a feature extractor or representation, so it cannot possess 'object-based features' that are subsequently 'overwritten.' I cross-referenced the methodology and found that the authors train the background ResNet18 (Places365-initialized) only on its final FC layer when fine-tuning on CIFAR-10. Their clear intent is to explain that freezing the backbone prevents the network from adapting its pretrained scene-centric representations to the object-centric statistical distribution of CIFAR-10. However, as written, the phrasing mistakenly attributes the features to the dataset itself, creating unnecessary ambiguity about what mechanism is actually being preserved.

comments.title: Underspecified regularization methodology due to missing equation
comments.quote:
The equation below regularized the foreground weights of the joint network with L2 penalty determined by a tuneable hyperparameter _α_ .
The first term was the standard cross entropy loss between the activation vector _ai_ and the target vector _ti_ for all N samples from C classes. The second term was the L2 penalty on the foreground weights, _θfg_ . This allowed the network to bias the classifier decision towards the background or the foreground based on the value of _α_ .
comments.explanation: The actual mathematical formula for the regularized loss is missing due to an OCR conversion failure (present as an image in the source PDF). While the text accurately describes the components (CE loss and L2 penalty on foreground weights), the absence of the explicit equation leaves the role and scaling of the hyperparameter _α_ underspecified. Standard conventions would place _α_ as a weighting factor for the penalty term (e.g., _L_ = _L_CE_ + _α_ ||_θ_fg__||^2), but without the visual, it is impossible to verify if proper scaling, normalization, or other terms are omitted. This omission hinders reproducibility of the proposed defense mechanism.

comments.title: Typographical error in regularization terminology
comments.quote: Surprisingly, the joint network with higher regulation outperformed the adversarial retraining strategy at all levels of _ϵ_ .
comments.explanation: The word 'regulation' is used where 'regularization' is clearly intended. This is a typographical error that does not fundamentally alter the technical meaning but disrupts academic prose standards. It is easily fixable.

comments.title: Inconsistent training protocol for the foreground classifier
comments.quote: Only the 512 dimensional fully connected layer was finetuned for MS COCO Lin et al. (2015) or CIFAR-10 Krizhevsky & Hinton (2009)." vs "For the foreground classifier, we trained all layers of a Resnet18 on CIFAR-10, with weights initialized from a Resnet 18 pretrained on Imagenet.
comments.explanation: The authors' intent appears to be a consistent fine-tuning protocol across datasets, but the methodology and results sections directly contradict each other. In Section 2.1, the text explicitly states that only the 512D FC layer is fine-tuned. However, in Section 3.3, the authors describe training "all layers" for the CIFAR-10 foreground classifier. I initially suspected a terminology slip (e.g., "all layers" referring to the backbone + FC, but only FC trained), but the phrasing "trained all layers" in deep learning typically implies full fine-tuning. This leaves the exact experimental configuration for the CIFAR-10 results ambiguous, affecting reproducibility and comparison of model capacity.

comments.title: Contradictory logical condition in dataset filtering criteria
comments.quote: We pruned the MS COCO dataset for images with fewer than two instances (<=2) of a single object so as to minimise cases of co-occurring context.
comments.explanation: The phrase "fewer than two instances" mathematically implies ≤1, which directly conflicts with the parenthetical "(<=2)". Given the stated goal of minimizing co-occurring contexts (i.e., multiple objects in one image), the authors almost certainly intended to filter *out* images with 1 object, meaning they wanted to keep images with 2 or more instances (≥2). As written, the logical condition is self-contradictory and the exact filtering threshold remains unclear.

comments.title: Missing mathematical formulation for FGSM perturbation
comments.quote:
Following Goodfellow et al. (2014) we used the equation

where _ϵ_ is a small real number, _J_ is the loss as a function of the parameters ( _θ_ ), the input ( _x_ ), and the label _y_ .
comments.explanation: The authors correctly identify the variables for the FGSM attack, but the actual mathematical equation is completely absent from the text (likely an OCR or PDF extraction error). While the standard FGSM update rule is well-known ($\alpha = \epsilon \cdot \text{sign}(\nabla_x J(\theta, x, y))$), its absence prevents verification of implementation specifics, such as whether the gradient is taken with respect to the loss before or after softmax, or how input clipping is handled. This hinders precise reproducibility.

comments.title: Underspecified adversarial attack generation protocol
comments.quote: Adversarial images were created from the full original images using FGSM method Goodfellow et al. (2014) applied to the foreground network, and performance was tested with the foreground, background and joint networks.
comments.explanation: The authors state that FGSM was applied to the foreground network to generate perturbations, but it is unclear whether attacks were also generated for the background and joint classifiers. In adversarial robustness studies, it is standard practice to generate model-specific (white-box) attacks for each classifier, or to explicitly delineate between white-box and black-box (transfer) attacks. Without clarifying if cross-model attacks were generated or if the same foreground-generated attacks were applied to all models, the robustness evaluation for the background and joint classifiers is underspecified.
